prompt:
You have been given six documents (prepended above) from the same platform,
all dated 2026-06-18: three incident postmortems (payments-api, search-api,
notify-worker), the day's change-management log, a shared-infrastructure
overview, and the incident-channel transcript. Each postmortem was written by a
different team, and each states a DIFFERENT local root cause. Your job is a
cross-incident analysis: determine whether these incidents share a common
underlying cause and, if so, identify it with evidence drawn from the
postmortems, the change log, the infrastructure overview, and the transcript
together.

Write a short analysis (roughly 150-300 words) that:
  1. States whether the three incidents share a common trigger, and what it is,
     citing the specific timeline evidence that appears in EACH incident.
  2. Names the specific shared resource / configuration involved and exactly
     what changed about it.
  3. Notes what local cause each individual postmortem blamed, and why those
     teams each plausibly missed the common cause.

Ground every claim in the provided postmortems only; do not invent causes,
services, timestamps, or IDs. Put your analysis between <answer> and </answer>
tags.

documents.title: Postmortem INC-5501 - payments-api
documents.content:
# Postmortem INC-5501 - payments-api elevated checkout 5xx
Service: payments-api      Severity: SEV-2      Date: 2026-06-18 (times UTC)
Author: payments on-call    Status: reviewed

## Summary
Between 09:16 and 10:02, payments-api returned an elevated rate of HTTP 5xx on
the POST /checkout path, peaking at a 7.3% error rate at 09:31. End users
experienced failed card authorizations and retried checkouts. Revenue impact
was estimated at a low-single-digit percentage of the window's transactions.

## Impact
- /checkout 5xx peak 7.3% (baseline < 0.2%).
- ~4,100 checkout attempts failed and were surfaced to users as errors.
- No data loss; failed authorizations were not charged.

## Detection
Alerted at 09:19 by the checkout-error-rate SLO burn alert. On-call
acknowledged at 09:20.

## Stated root cause
Increased latency and timeouts from our downstream card processor, PayGate.
PayGate's p99 response time rose during the window and our client calls to it
exceeded the 3s client timeout, which surfaced to callers as 5xx. The PayGate
status page later confirmed elevated latency on their side during a portion of
the window. We have opened a vendor ticket.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 09:14  config-sync applied change set CS-2288 (a fleet-wide configuration
         rollout). Routine; not initially suspected.
- 09:16  first elevated 5xx on /checkout. PayGate call latency begins climbing.
- 09:19  SLO burn alert fires; on-call paged.
- 09:22  PayGate dashboard shows raised p99; we focus the investigation there.
- 09:25  we observe that our own datastore connection-pool acquisition is slow
         (requests waiting for a connection) but read this as downstream
         backpressure from the PayGate slowness holding request handlers open.
- 09:34  mitigation: we raise the PayGate client timeout from 3s to 6s. Partial
         relief - fewer hard 5xx, but latency stays high and pool acquisition
         is still slow.
- 09:47  secondary mitigation to the pool acquire timeout; little effect.
- 10:02  PayGate latency returns to normal per their status page and our
         /checkout error rate returns to baseline at the same time.

## Contributing factors
- No circuit breaker around PayGate calls, so their latency mapped directly to
  our error rate.
- The 3s client timeout was tuned for PayGate's normal latency, not a spike.

## Action items
- [ ] Escalate the PayGate latency incident with the vendor.
- [ ] Add a circuit breaker around PayGate calls.
- [ ] Add a dashboard panel correlating PayGate p99 with our /checkout errors.

## Notes
Connection-pool acquisition to the SHARED datastore was slow during the window.
We assumed this was a symptom of PayGate backpressure (handlers held open
waiting on PayGate), not an independent cause, and did not investigate the pool
itself.

## Five whys (payments team analysis)
1. Why did /checkout return 5xx? Calls to PayGate exceeded the 3s timeout.
2. Why did PayGate calls exceed the timeout? PayGate p99 latency rose, per
   their status page.
3. Why did our error rate track PayGate so tightly? No circuit breaker isolates
   us from downstream latency.
4. Why did datastore pool acquisition also slow down? We assumed handlers were
   held open waiting on PayGate, consuming pooled connections. (We did not
   check whether the pool's configured size had changed - in hindsight a gap.)
5. Why did the timeout bump only partially help? Because latency, not a hard
   failure, was the proximate symptom. We stopped here and attributed the
   incident to PayGate.

## Related signals (not fully investigated)
- Datastore pool acquisition-wait p99 rose from ~2ms to >900ms at 09:16 and
  recovered around 10:01. We did not chase the 10:01 recovery time.
- Other services reportedly had issues the same morning; we did not correlate.

## What went well / poorly
- Well: fast detection via the SLO burn alert.
- Poorly: we anchored on the vendor and never questioned the shared datastore
  pool, so our action items are all PayGate-shaped.

## Appendix: metrics and log excerpts
Key dashboard readings during the window (1-minute resolution):
  time   checkout_5xx%   paygate_p99_ms   db_pool_acquire_wait_p99_ms
  09:13     0.1             410               2
  09:16     3.9            1120             430
  09:22     6.4            1180             770
  09:31     7.3            1240             910
  09:40     5.1            1090             880
  09:52     2.7             930             540
  10:02     0.2             430               3
Note the db_pool_acquire_wait_p99 tracks the checkout_5xx curve at least as
closely as paygate_p99 does; both rise at 09:16 and both fall back only at
10:02. We did not weigh the pool-wait signal at the time.

Representative application log lines (payments-api):
  09:16:04 WARN  db.pool acquire wait 431ms (pool at capacity)
  09:22:11 WARN  db.pool acquire wait 774ms (pool at capacity)
  09:22:12 ERROR paygate call timeout after 3000ms; returning 502
  09:34:50 INFO  raised paygate client timeout to 6000ms
  09:41:33 WARN  db.pool acquire wait 860ms (pool at capacity)
  10:02:08 INFO  db.pool acquire wait 3ms; error rate normalizing

## Customer communications
Status page updated at 09:28 ("elevated checkout errors, investigating") and
resolved at 10:07. Support handled ~30 tickets about failed payments.

## Remediation status
- PayGate circuit breaker: design in review.
- PayGate escalation: vendor ticket open; they acknowledged elevated latency
  but could not fully account for our window - a loose end we did not pursue.

## Retrospective note (added after cross-incident review)
Two facts we under-weighted at the time: (1) the db_pool_acquire_wait_p99
signal tracked our error rate as tightly as PayGate's p99 did, rising at 09:16
and only clearing at 10:02; and (2) PayGate themselves could not fully account
for the depth of our impact from their side. Both point away from a
PayGate-only story. The vendor's latency was real but modest; the shared
connection pool being starved is what turned it into a 7.3% error rate. We
recorded PayGate as the root cause because it was the first plausible external
signal and we lacked visibility into fleet-wide configuration changes during
the incident. The cross-incident review connects this incident to INC-5507 and
INC-5512; see the change-management log for the shared change that the three
incidents have in common.

documents.title: Postmortem INC-5507 - search-api
documents.content:
# Postmortem INC-5507 - search-api p99 latency spike
Service: search-api      Severity: SEV-2      Date: 2026-06-18 (times UTC)
Author: search on-call    Status: reviewed

## Summary
From 09:15 to 10:00, search-api p99 latency rose from a baseline of ~180ms to
a peak of 4.2s, and a fraction of queries timed out at the 3s gateway limit.
Search remained available but visibly degraded; the autocomplete and results
pages were slow.

## Impact
- p99 latency 180ms -> 4.2s at peak (09:33).
- ~2.1% of search queries timed out during the window.
- No incorrect results returned; purely a latency/timeout event.

## Detection
Alerted at 09:21 by the search-latency SLO alert. On-call acknowledged 09:23.

## Stated root cause
An expensive query plan. An overnight index rebuild on the search corpus had
completed that morning, and we believe the query planner subsequently chose a
poor plan for a hot query shape, causing sequential scans under load. Running
ANALYZE to refresh planner statistics restored plan quality and latency.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 08:40  overnight index rebuild on the search corpus completes successfully.
- 09:14  config-sync rolls out change set CS-2288 across the fleet. Noted in
         the deploy feed; not initially connected to the incident.
- 09:15  p99 latency begins climbing; the slow-query log starts filling.
- 09:16  repeated "connection acquisition timeout" errors appear in the logs
         when talking to the SHARED pgbouncer connection pool; queries queue
         waiting for a database connection.
- 09:21  SLO alert fires; on-call paged.
- 09:26  we hypothesise a bad plan following the index rebuild and prepare an
         ANALYZE.
- 09:30  we run ANALYZE on the hot table; plan quality improves, but p99 stays
         elevated and the connection-acquisition timeouts continue.
- 09:52  only partial improvement; queries still queue waiting for a connection.
- 10:00  p99 returns to normal and the acquisition timeouts cease.

## Contributing factors
- The index rebuild changed table statistics, making a plan regression
  plausible and drawing our attention.
- Alerting did not distinguish "slow query" from "waiting for a connection".

## Action items
- [ ] Add a query hint / plan guard for the hot query shape.
- [ ] Alert on slow-query-log growth rate.
- [ ] Add a distinct alert for connection-acquisition timeouts vs query time.

## Notes
The connection-acquisition timeouts logged at 09:16 were noticed but attributed
to the query pile-up holding pooled connections open longer than usual. We did
not examine the pool's configured size.

## Five whys (search team analysis)
1. Why did p99 spike? Some queries ran far slower than usual.
2. Why were queries slow? We believed the planner chose a bad plan for a hot
   query shape.
3. Why would the plan regress now? An overnight index rebuild had refreshed
   statistics; a plan flip seemed plausible and timely.
4. Why did ANALYZE appear to fix it? Re-running statistics can flip the plan
   back - though the timing also coincides with an unrelated fleet change we
   did not weigh at the time.
5. Why did we see connection-acquisition timeouts at 09:16? We assumed slow
   queries were holding pooled connections; we did not check the pool ceiling.
   We stopped here and attributed the incident to the query plan.

## Related signals (not fully investigated)
- pgbouncer "connection acquisition timeout" count spiked at 09:16 and fell to
  zero shortly after 10:00. We did not investigate why it cleared then.
- The ANALYZE at 09:30 and recovery at 09:49 may be partly coincidental; the
  acquisition-timeout signal, not query time, dominated the early window.

## What went well / poorly
- Well: ANALYZE is a cheap, reversible mitigation and we tried it quickly.
- Poorly: the index rebuild was a compelling but possibly misleading lead; it
  pulled attention away from the shared connection pool.

## Appendix: metrics and log excerpts
Key dashboard readings during the window (1-minute resolution):
  time   search_p99_ms   pgbouncer_wait_ms   pgbouncer_acquire_timeouts_per_min
  09:14     185               3                  0
  09:16    1400             520                 42
  09:26    3900             910                 88
  09:33    4200             980                101
  09:41    2600             870                 74
  09:52    1300             610                 39
  10:00     190               4                  0
The pgbouncer acquisition-timeout rate rises and falls with the latency and,
like it, only returns to zero at 10:00 - after, not because of, the ANALYZE at
09:30. We noted but did not act on this ordering.

Representative log lines (search-api):
  09:16:20 WARN  pgbouncer: connection acquisition timeout after 500ms
  09:26:41 WARN  pgbouncer: connection acquisition timeout after 500ms
  09:30:05 INFO  ran ANALYZE on hot table; plan cost estimate improved
  09:52:19 WARN  pgbouncer: connection acquisition timeout after 500ms
  10:00:12 INFO  pgbouncer: acquisitions healthy; p99 normalizing

## Customer communications
No public status-page entry (search stayed available, just slow). Internal
stakeholders were notified at 09:35.

## Remediation status
- Hot-query plan hint deployed (CS-2291) as a guard.
- Distinct alert for acquisition timeouts vs query time: not yet built. Had it
  existed, it would likely have pointed at the pool rather than the planner.

## Retrospective note (added after cross-incident review)
In hindsight the timing is telling: the acquisition-timeout signal began at
09:16, one minute after the config-sync rollout window opened, and it did not
clear when we ran ANALYZE at 09:30 - it cleared at 10:00, alongside the two
other incidents that morning. Our ANALYZE was a plausible but likely incidental
mitigation. We recorded the query-plan story as the root cause because it was
the most legible local explanation available to us during the incident, and we
did not have visibility into fleet-wide configuration changes at the time. See
the cross-incident review for how this incident related to INC-5501 and
INC-5512.

documents.title: Postmortem INC-5512 - notify-worker
documents.content:
# Postmortem INC-5512 - notify-worker notification backlog
Service: notify-worker    Severity: SEV-3      Date: 2026-06-18 (times UTC)
Author: notifications on-call    Status: reviewed (with config-audit addendum)

## Summary
A backlog of approximately 40,000 notifications accumulated between 09:15 and
10:05. No notifications were lost; the backlog fully drained once datastore
throughput recovered. User-visible effect was delayed emails/pushes (up to
~45 minutes late at the peak).

## Impact
- Peak queue depth ~40,000 (baseline < 500).
- Notification delivery delayed up to ~45 min.
- No data loss.

## Detection
Queue-depth alert at 09:22; on-call acknowledged 09:24.

## Stated root cause
Slow consumer. We observed long garbage-collection pauses on two worker nodes
and concluded the consumers could not keep up, so the queue backed up. Rolling
the two worst-offending workers cleared the backlog.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 09:15  "db pool exhausted" errors begin: workers block trying to acquire a
         SHARED datastore connection to mark notifications as sent.
- 09:18  throughput of the "mark sent" step collapses; queue depth climbs.
- 09:22  queue-depth alert fires; on-call paged.
- 09:29  we observe long GC pauses on two workers and suspect slow consumers.
- 09:40  we roll the two workers with the longest GC pauses.
- 09:55  "db pool exhausted" errors subside; throughput recovers.
- 10:05  backlog fully drained.

## Contributing factors
- Two workers did show real GC pressure, which drew our attention and appeared
  to confirm the slow-consumer theory.
- The "db pool exhausted" errors were logged at WARN and not alerted on.

## Config-audit addendum (added during incident review)
During review, a config auditor flagged change set CS-2288 - applied at 09:14
by config-sync - as the suspicious event of that morning: it touched the SHARED
datastore setting `db.pool.maxSize`. See the day's change-management log for the
exact before/after values, the realised scope, and the revert time. The auditor
noted that the GC pauses were real but were unlikely to be the trigger on their
own, because the "db pool exhausted" errors began at 09:15, before the GC pauses
were observed at 09:29. We have not yet reconciled this addendum with the stated
root cause above; that reconciliation is an open action item.

## Action items
- [ ] Reconcile the config-audit addendum (CS-2288 / db.pool.maxSize) with the
      stated slow-consumer root cause; the two may not be independent.
- [ ] Add a guardrail so pool-sizing changes cannot be rolled out fleet-wide by
      default (require an explicit fleet-scope flag).
- [ ] Promote "db pool exhausted" from WARN to a paging alert.
- [ ] Investigate the worker GC pauses SEPARATELY (real, but not this incident).

## Five whys (notify team analysis, pre-addendum)
1. Why did notifications back up? The "mark sent" step's throughput collapsed.
2. Why did throughput collapse? Workers blocked acquiring a datastore
   connection ("db pool exhausted").
3. Why were connections exhausted? At the time we assumed the consumers were
   slow (GC pauses) and holding connections.
4. Why did rolling the workers help? It cleared the GC-heavy processes - though
   recovery also coincides with an unrelated fleet change (see addendum).
5. Why did "db pool exhausted" start at 09:15, before we saw GC pauses at 09:29?
   Unexplained at the time; the addendum above suggests the pool ceiling itself
   changed. This is the open reconciliation item.

## Appendix: metrics and log excerpts
Key dashboard readings during the window (1-minute resolution):
  time   queue_depth   mark_sent_tps   db_pool_exhausted_errs_per_min   worker_gc_pause_ms_p99
  09:14      480           210               0                              120
  09:15      620           150              38                              130
  09:22    12400            60             140                              180
  09:29    26800            40             160                              540
  09:40    33900            55             150                              520
  09:55    18200           140              44                              210
  10:05      420           205               1                              150
The db_pool_exhausted error rate jumps at 09:15 - fourteen minutes before the
GC-pause p99 climbs at 09:29 - and only clears after 09:55, trending to zero by
10:05. The ordering argues against GC pauses being the trigger.

Representative log lines (notify-worker):
  09:15:03 WARN  datastore: db pool exhausted; waiting for a connection
  09:15:44 WARN  datastore: db pool exhausted; waiting for a connection
  09:29:10 WARN  gc: pause 540ms on worker-7
  09:40:22 INFO  rolled worker-7 and worker-12 (highest GC pauses)
  09:55:31 INFO  datastore: connections available; drain accelerating
  10:05:12 INFO  backlog cleared

## Customer communications
No public status entry; delayed notifications were delivered late rather than
dropped. A follow-up email explained the delay to affected internal teams.

## Remediation status
- Fleet-scope guardrail for pool-sizing changes: filed, high priority.
- "db pool exhausted" promoted to a paging alert.
- GC investigation tracked separately (JIRA NOTIFY-3391): real, but not this
  incident's trigger.

documents.title: Change-Management Log - 2026-06-18
documents.content:
# Change-Management Log - 2026-06-18 (config-sync, all times UTC)

Auto-generated ledger of every change set applied by config-sync on this date.
Each row: change-set id, apply time, author/team, intended scope, summary.
Rows are in apply-time order. This ledger records INTENT and does not by itself
show realised blast radius.

- CS-2280  06:02  platform    scope=global   Rotate internal TLS bundle.
- CS-2281  06:40  search      scope=search   Bump search cache TTL 300->600s.
- CS-2282  07:15  payments    scope=payments Add PayGate retry jitter 50ms.
- CS-2283  07:48  platform    scope=global   Enable access-log sampling 0.1.
- CS-2284  08:05  data        scope=global   Add read-replica endpoint to DSN.
- CS-2285  08:33  notify      scope=notify   Raise notify worker concurrency.
- CS-2286  08:51  search      scope=search   Post-index-rebuild planner reset.
- CS-2287  09:03  platform    scope=global   Tighten ingress rate-limit to 8k rps.
- CS-2288  09:14  data        scope=canary   Connection-pool sizing template
           update: set db.pool.maxSize=20 on the canary host for a soak test.
           [Templating note: the scope flag was not honoured; see incident
           review addenda for realised effect.]
- CS-2289  09:20  payments    scope=payments Raise PayGate client timeout 3s->6s.
- CS-2290  09:26  platform    scope=global   Add trace exemplar sampling.
- CS-2291  09:31  search      scope=search   Deploy hot-query plan hint.
- CS-2292  09:44  notify      scope=notify   Roll two notify workers (GC).
- CS-2293  09:58  data        scope=global   Add slow-connection log line.
- CS-2294  10:01  data        scope=global   Restore db.pool.maxSize=200 fleet-wide
           (revert of the realised effect of CS-2288).
- CS-2295  10:22  platform    scope=global   Re-enable ingress rate-limit to 12k.

Reviewer note appended 2026-06-19: CS-2288 was authored as a canary-only soak
but a templating defect caused config-sync to apply db.pool.maxSize=20 (down
from the normal 200) FLEET-WIDE at 09:14, not just on the canary. It was
reverted by CS-2294 at 10:01, restoring db.pool.maxSize to 200. The realised
fleet-wide window (09:14-10:01) coincides with three SEV incidents the same
morning (payments, search, notify), each of which independently reported
connection-pool exhaustion or acquisition timeouts in that window.

## Detailed notes on selected change sets
- CS-2282 / CS-2289 (payments): both touch PayGate client behaviour (retry
  jitter, then the 3s->6s timeout bump during the incident). Neither changes a
  shared resource; blast radius is payments-only.
- CS-2286 / CS-2291 (search): planner-stats reset after the index rebuild, then
  a hot-query plan hint. Search-scoped; do not touch the shared pool.
- CS-2285 / CS-2292 (notify): a concurrency bump earlier, then a worker roll
  during the incident. Notify-scoped.
- CS-2288 (data, intended canary): the ONLY change set on this date that
  altered a SHARED data-tier setting (db.pool.maxSize). Its intended scope was
  a single canary host; its realised scope was the fleet (templating defect).
  This is the only same-day change with fleet-wide blast radius on a shared
  resource, which is why the config audit centred on it.
- CS-2294 (data): the revert; restored db.pool.maxSize=200 fleet-wide at 10:01.

## Scope-flag audit (added 2026-06-19)
Of the 16 change sets applied on 2026-06-18, 15 realised the scope they
declared. CS-2288 is the sole exception: declared scope=canary, realised
scope=fleet. The templating helper that expands host selectors dropped the
canary predicate for pool-sizing templates specifically; other template
families were unaffected, which is why only CS-2288 mis-scoped. A guardrail
requiring an explicit `--fleet` acknowledgement for shared-tier settings has
been filed as the top remediation across all three incident reviews.

documents.title: Shared Infrastructure Overview
documents.content:
# Shared Infrastructure Overview (platform wiki excerpt)

This page documents the shared data-access tier used by most stateful services
on the platform. It is background reference, not incident-specific.

## Shared connection pooling (pgbouncer tier)
Most services do NOT connect to the primary datastore directly. They connect
through a SHARED pgbouncer connection-pooling tier. The pooler's upper bound on
concurrent server-side connections is governed by the config-managed setting
`db.pool.maxSize`. Because the tier is shared, `db.pool.maxSize` is a GLOBAL
resource ceiling: if it is lowered, EVERY service that routes through the tier
competes for fewer connections at once, and connection acquisition can time out
across unrelated services simultaneously.

Services that route through the shared pgbouncer tier include (non-exhaustive):
payments-api, search-api, notify-worker, ledger-api, and profile-api. A change
to `db.pool.maxSize` therefore has fleet-wide blast radius unless it is scoped
to a specific host.

## Normal sizing
The steady-state value of `db.pool.maxSize` is 200. This comfortably serves the
aggregate connection demand of the services above at normal traffic. Values far
below steady state (for example, an order of magnitude smaller) will starve the
pool: callers block on acquisition, time out, and surface the failure in
service-specific ways - 5xx here, latency there, a queue backlog elsewhere -
even though the underlying cause is a single shared ceiling.

## Symptoms of pool starvation (how it looks per service)
- A request-serving API: elevated 5xx or latency, "pool at capacity" /
  "acquire wait" warnings.
- A query-heavy API: latency spikes and "connection acquisition timeout" logs
  that can be mistaken for slow queries.
- A background worker: throughput collapse and "db pool exhausted" errors, which
  can be mistaken for a slow consumer.
Because the surface symptom differs by service, independent teams can each
diagnose a different LOCAL cause for what is actually one shared event.

## Change management for shared settings
Changes to `db.pool.maxSize` (and other shared-tier settings) are delivered by
config-sync and MUST carry an explicit scope. A change intended for a single
canary host that is mis-scoped to the fleet will move the global ceiling for
everyone. See the change-management log for the authoritative record of what
was applied on any given day.

## Capacity math (why 20 starves the fleet)
At normal traffic the services on the shared tier hold, in aggregate, roughly
120-150 concurrent server-side connections at peak, against the steady-state
ceiling of 200 - comfortable headroom. If the ceiling is lowered to 20, demand
exceeds supply by nearly an order of magnitude: at most 20 connections can be
checked out fleet-wide at once, and every additional caller blocks on
acquisition until one frees. The result is simultaneous acquisition timeouts
and pool-exhausted errors across ALL services on the tier, with each service's
request pattern turning the shortage into its own characteristic symptom.

## Per-service connection profile (approximate, at peak)
  - payments-api:   ~40 connections (short, bursty; sensitive to acquisition
    latency because /checkout is synchronous).
  - search-api:     ~35 connections (query-bound; acquisition stalls look like
    slow queries).
  - notify-worker:  ~30 connections (steady; a shortfall becomes a backlog).
  - ledger-api, profile-api: the remainder.
A ceiling of 20 cannot satisfy even a single one of these services at peak, let
alone all of them, which is why a fleet-wide drop to 20 produces coincident,
differently-shaped incidents rather than one localized failure.

## Diagnosing shared-pool events
The tell-tale that an incident is a shared-pool event, not a local one, is that
the pool-acquisition / pool-exhausted signal appears in MULTIPLE unrelated
services within the same minutes, and clears in all of them at the same time -
typically when a shared-setting change is reverted. A single service's local
problem does not produce synchronized pool signals across the fleet.

## Shared-pool incident forensics playbook
When several services degrade at once, run this checklist BEFORE accepting any
single team's local root cause:
  1. Collect the pool-acquisition / pool-exhausted signal from each affected
     service and overlay them on one timeline. Synchronized onset (same minute)
     is the first red flag.
  2. Pull the change-management log for the window. Filter for any change set
     that touched a SHARED-tier setting (db.pool.*, pgbouncer.*). A single such
     change with fleet realised-scope is the prime suspect.
  3. Compare each incident's recovery time to the revert time of that change.
     Recovery clustered at the revert is strong corroboration; recovery that
     each team attributes to its own local fix, yet all lands at the same
     minute, is a sign the local fixes were incidental.
  4. Check the declared vs realised scope of the suspect change. A canary-
     declared change that realised fleet-wide is the classic shared-pool
     incident.
  5. Only then weigh the local causes. Local factors (a real GC pause, a genuine
     vendor blip, a plausible plan flip) are often PRESENT and act as red
     herrings; presence of a plausible local cause does not rule out a shared
     trigger.

## Worked pattern (generic)
Service A (synchronous API) shows 5xx and blames a downstream vendor whose
latency did rise a little. Service B (query API) shows latency and blames a
plan regression after a recent index change. Service C (worker) shows a backlog
and blames slow consumers / GC. Each is locally plausible. If all three ALSO
show pool-acquisition errors starting in the same minute and clearing together
at a config revert, the shared pool ceiling - not any of the three local
stories - is the common root cause. The local stories explain the SHAPE of each
incident; the shared change explains why all three happened at once.

## Why local causes are seductive here
Each service has a REAL, standing local risk that a shared-pool event lights
up: payments genuinely depends on a flaky vendor; search genuinely just did an
index rebuild; notify genuinely has GC pressure on some nodes. A shared pool
starvation makes each of these standing risks LOOK like the cause, because it
degrades each service through the path that service already worries about. That
is precisely why three good teams can each write a confident, internally
coherent, and WRONG root-cause section on the same morning. The discipline that
catches it is refusing to close a multi-service event on a single-service cause
until the shared-tier change log has been checked.

## See also
- The change-management log records every config change and its realised scope.
- Each incident postmortem records its team's local root cause and its timeline;
  cross-reference the pool-signal timings across them.

documents.title: Incident channel transcript - 2026-06-18
documents.content:
# Incident channel transcript - #inc-2026-06-18 (times UTC, lightly redacted)

This is the shared incident channel for the morning of 2026-06-18. Three teams
responded to what they each believed were separate incidents. The transcript is
included for context; no one connected the incidents during the response.

09:17  payments_oncall: checkout 5xx is climbing, looks like PayGate is slow.
       p99 to them is way up. Opening INC-5501.
09:19  search_oncall: unrelated (?) but search p99 just spiked too. We had an
       index rebuild finish overnight, might be a bad plan. Opening INC-5507.
09:20  platform_oncall: heads up, config-sync pushed a batch of change sets this
       morning (CS-2280..CS-2288). Anything look related? Most are scoped to
       individual teams.
09:21  payments_oncall: our thing is definitely PayGate, their status page shows
       elevated latency. Bumping our client timeout.
09:23  search_oncall: seeing "connection acquisition timeout" to pgbouncer too,
       but probably just the slow queries hogging connections. Running ANALYZE.
09:24  notify_oncall: notify queue is backing up, ~12k and climbing. Workers are
       throwing "db pool exhausted". Might be GC on a couple of nodes.
09:25  platform_oncall: three services at once is a lot of coincidence. Are we
       sure these are separate?
09:26  payments_oncall: ours is PayGate, pretty sure. Different symptom from
       search.
09:26  search_oncall: ours is the query plan after the rebuild. Different from
       notify.
09:27  notify_oncall: ours is slow consumers / GC. Rolling the worst workers.
09:31  platform_oncall: CS-2288 was a db pool sizing change tagged canary. Anyone
       on the canary host? Probably not relevant if it's canary-scoped.
09:33  search_oncall: ANALYZE done, plan looks better, but p99 still high and
       still getting acquisition timeouts. Watching.
09:41  payments_oncall: timeout bump helped a bit, still elevated. PayGate still
       slow on their end.
09:52  notify_oncall: rolled two workers, backlog still draining slowly, still
       some pool-exhausted errors.
10:00  search_oncall: p99 back to normal now, acquisition timeouts stopped.
       Calling INC-5507 mitigated. Crediting the ANALYZE.
10:02  payments_oncall: checkout back to baseline, PayGate normalized. INC-5501
       resolved.
10:05  notify_oncall: backlog cleared. INC-5512 resolved. Filing GC follow-up.
10:12  platform_oncall: weird that all three cleared within a few minutes of each
       other. Flagging CS-2288 for a config audit just in case - it touched a
       shared setting. Will follow up async.
10:40  platform_oncall: (async) config audit pending; will append findings to
       INC-5512 since that's the one with the clearest pool signal.

--- later that day, async in the same channel ---

13:05  platform_oncall: config audit update. Pulled the change-management log
       for today. Of the 16 change sets, 15 realised their declared scope. One
       did not.
13:06  platform_oncall: CS-2288 declared scope=canary but a templating defect
       applied it fleet-wide. It set the shared db.pool.maxSize down from its
       normal value. That's a global connection ceiling for everything on the
       shared pooler.
13:07  search_oncall: ...oh. That would explain the acquisition timeouts we
       blamed on the query plan. The ANALYZE probably didn't fix anything.
13:08  payments_oncall: and our "PayGate" pool-wait signal. The timeout bump was
       treating a symptom.
13:09  notify_oncall: and our "slow consumer" theory. The pool-exhausted errors
       started before the GC pauses. It lines up.
13:12  platform_oncall: exact before/after values, the fleet-wide scope, and the
       revert time are in the change-management log; I'll write the specifics
       into the INC-5512 addendum so there's one canonical record. Net: one
       shared change, three differently-shaped incidents, three separate
       root-cause calls that each missed it.
13:15  platform_oncall: action item across all three reviews: a guardrail so a
       shared-tier setting can't be pushed fleet-wide without an explicit
       acknowledgement. Filing now.

checker.rubric:
# Blind grading rubric - R3 (cross-incident root-cause synthesis)

Score each numbered criterion independently: FULL points if fully met, HALF if
partially met, ZERO if not met or contradicted. Sum to a total out of 10.
Skeptic-first: enumerate what is missing per criterion before crediting it;
when torn, round down. This task PASSES iff total >= 7/10.

1. (2 pts) Identifies that all three incidents share a common trigger at about
   09:14-09:16 UTC: the config-sync rollout of change set CS-2288, and cites
   that CS-2288 (or the 09:14 config-sync rollout) appears in EACH incident's
   timeline.
2. (2 pts) Names the specific shared change: CS-2288 lowered the shared
   datastore connection-pool maximum `db.pool.maxSize` from 200 to 20
   fleet-wide, exhausting the pool shared across services (restored to 200 at
   ~10:01, matching the recovery times).
3. (2 pts) Notes that each postmortem blamed a DIFFERENT local cause -
   payments-api: PayGate / downstream processor latency; search-api: an
   expensive query plan / index rebuild; notify-worker: a slow consumer / GC
   pauses - and thus each team individually missed the shared root cause.
4. (2 pts) Enumerates the three incidents correctly (INC-5501 payments-api,
   INC-5507 search-api, INC-5512 notify-worker); every claim is attributable to
   a provided postmortem, with no fabricated incidents, services, or numbers.
5. (2 pts) Grounds the common-cause claim in the actual evidence (the shared
   CS-2288 timeline entries plus the pool-exhaustion / connection-acquisition
   symptoms and the recovery-on-restore timing) rather than asserting an
   unsupported cause; treats the shared timestamp as corroborated by the
   pool-exhaustion symptoms, not as bare coincidence.
